label: Sample pack — glycine / l-theanine / taurine (docs example)

substances.name: Glycine
substances.description: A non-essential amino acid used by the body as a building block for protein and collagen, and commonly discussed in the context of evening relaxation routines.
substances.origin: Naturally occurring amino acid; found in gelatin, collagen, and many protein-rich foods.
substances.how_obtained: Synthesized commercially or extracted from collagen/gelatin sources.
substances.half_life: Estimates vary; not firmly established in humans.
substances.reported_dose_range: Commonly studied range: 3 g, often taken shortly before bed
health_risks: Mild gastrointestinal discomfort reported at high doses
subjective_effects: Reported reduction in time to fall asleep

substances.name: L-Theanine
substances.description: An amino acid found in tea leaves that is commonly discussed for calm focus, often alongside caffeine.
substances.origin: Naturally occurring amino acid found in tea leaves (Camellia sinensis) and some mushrooms.
substances.how_obtained: Extracted from tea or produced via fermentation/synthesis.
health_risks: No significant risks reported in reviewed literature
subjective_effects: Reported reduced jitteriness when paired with caffeine

substances.name: Taurine
substances.description: A naturally occurring amino sulfonic acid found in meat, fish, and dairy, and produced endogenously; commonly discussed in the context of energy drinks and exercise performance.
substances.origin: Naturally occurring amino sulfonic acid found in meat, fish, and dairy; also produced endogenously.
substances.half_life: Estimates vary; not firmly established in humans.
substances.reported_dose_range: Commonly discussed range: 500 mg-2 g daily
subjective_effects: Reported reduced perceived fatigue during exercise

brands.name: Purity Labs
brands.description: A supplement brand focused on single-ingredient amino acid products with published certificates of analysis.

stacks.name: Evening Amino Stack
stacks.description: A combination of glycine, L-theanine, and taurine that appears in user-shared evening-routine discussions; included here as an example, not a recommendation.

sources.title: Effects of L-Theanine Administration on Stress-Related Symptoms and Cognitive Functions in Healthy Adults: A Randomized Controlled Trial
sources.authors: Hidese S, Ogawa S, Ota M, Ishida I, Yasukawa Z, Ozeki M, Kunugi H
sources.notes: Randomized, placebo-controlled, crossover trial; primary source for the sleep-quality finding in this pack.

sources.title: New Therapeutic Strategy for Amino Acid Medicine: Glycine Improves the Quality of Sleep
sources.notes: Review summarizing human and animal glycine/sleep research; primary source for the sleep-quality finding in this pack.

findings.endpoint: self-reported sleep quality (Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index score)
findings.finding_summary: In a 2019 randomized, placebo-controlled, crossover trial of 30 healthy adults, four weeks of 200 mg/day L-theanine was associated with improved Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index scores, including subscales for sleep latency and sleep disturbance, compared with placebo.
findings.population: 30 healthy adults (9 men, 21 women; mean age approximately 48 years), no major psychiatric illness
findings.limitations: Small sample size; self-reported outcome measures; crossover design may carry residual effects between phases.

findings.finding_summary: A 2012 review of human trials summarized findings that glycine taken shortly before bedtime was associated with improved next-day alertness and subjective sleep quality ratings in adults reporting unsatisfactory sleep, alongside proposed mechanisms involving core body temperature regulation.
findings.population: Adults with self-reported unsatisfactory sleep (trials summarized in the review)
findings.limitations: Narrative review summarizing multiple small trials rather than a single controlled study; some proposed mechanisms are based on animal data.
